claim: Handoff skill enables compressing context from one AI session into a markdown file for clean handoff to another session, enabling parallel work without diluting context
actionable: Create a handoff.md using /handoff skill to save current session state; pass to a new agent to work on out-of-scope tasks in parallel without clobbering original session progress

claim: Early tokens in LLM context window receive better attention (less diffuse) than later tokens; best performance is around 120K tokens despite larger advertised context windows
actionable: Budget your context window usage carefully; aim to stay in the 'smart zone' (under ~120K tokens) for best reasoning; use /compact or /handoff when approaching this limit

claim: Grilling sessions (iterative planning/brainstorming with agents) should remain pure and focused; use handoff to delegate tangential work (bug fixes, refactoring) to separate agents
actionable: During planning sessions, identify out-of-scope tasks and use /handoff to delegate them; this sharpens the current session and enables parallel work streams

claim: Handoff-to-prototype pattern enables exploring uncertain UI/logic in a separate prototype session, then passing learnings back to grilling session for planning decisions
actionable: During planning, identify unknowns requiring prototyping; handoff with instructions to prototype; prototype learns reality; create new handoff with learnings back to planner

claim: Handoff documents should include suggested skills section so next agent automatically invokes needed skills without extra instruction
actionable: When creating handoff documents, include a 'Suggested Skills' section listing relevant skills (/grill-with-docs, /prototype, etc.) so the receiving agent knows what to use

claim: Handoff documents should use pointers and avoid duplicating content already in artifacts, files, or resources like GitHub issues to keep them maintainable
actionable: Keep handoff.md concise by referencing external resources (GitHub issues, existing docs) rather than repeating information; use links and file references
